english: for something, grágs-pa-la (grags pa la) for glory; to be attached to, to cling to, e.g. lus (lus) dań (dang) sróg-la (srog la) čágs-pa (chags pa) to life, yúl-la (yul la) to one's home, to one's native country; often: to suffer one's self to be enticed by a thing, to indulge in; čágs-par (chags par) mi (mi) bya (bya) ǰígs-par ('jigs par) mi (mi) byá-ste (bya ste) allowing neither desire nor fear to have any influence upon himself Samb. — 2. sbst. love (ἔρως), lust, passion for, affection, attachment, čágs-pa (chags pa) skyés-so (skyes so) he fell in love Dzl.; čágs-pa (chags pa) spyód-pa (spyod pa) = krig-pa (khrig pa) spyód-pa (spyod pa). According to Buddhistic theory all čágs-pa (chags pa) is a great evil, as it betrays a troubled state of mind, and a reprehensible attachment to external things; yet even a saint, so far advanced in dispassion and apathy as Milaraspa, may sometimes be caught in very tender affections and sensations of čágs-pa (chags pa), very like those of other human creatures. Comp. čags-sdáń (chags sdang) 1. Schr. love and hatred, 2. Glr., Pth. jealousy (love showing itself in hatred), also čags-sdáń-gi (chags sdang gi) prág-dog (phrag dog). — čags-spyód (chags spyod) coition, copulation, cf. čágs-pa (chags pa) III., 2. — čags-źén (chags zhen), also źen-čágs (zhen chags) = čágs-pa (chags pa) sbst. Mil.; *čags-źén (chags zhen) čò-če* (co ce) W., to love, c. la (la); čags-źén (chags zhen) méd-pa (med pa) dispassionate, indifferent to all terrestrial things. — čags-séms (chags sems) = čags-źén (chags zhen). — čágs-sred-čań (chags sred can) Pth. lustful, libidinous, wanton.

resp. skyems (skyems), gsól-čań (gsol chang), mčód-čań (mchod chang) C., a fermented liquor, beer, wine, (not 'brandy' Sch.); bu (bu) skyés-pa-la (skyes pa la) miń (ming), čań (chang) dráńs-pa-la (drangs pa la) gtam (gtam) proverb: to the new-born child a name (is due), to the beer to be drunk a talk; nás-čań (nas chang) beer made of barley (the usual kind); brás-čań ('bras chang) of rice Glr.; gró-čań (gro chang) of wheat Cs.; bú-ram-čań (bu ram chang), or búr-čań (bur chang) of sugar Med.; rgún-čań (rgun chang) wine; sbráń-čań (sbrang chang) Med. honey-wine, mulse, mead? rús-čań (rus chang) Med.? — zás-čań (zas chang), zán-čań (zan chang) eating and drinking, meat and drink. — sloń- (slong-), tig- (tig-), and bsu-čań (bsu chang) v. sub bág-ma (bag ma). — Fig.: btúń-ba (btung ba) dran-śes (dran shes) bdúd-rtsii (bdud rtsii) čań (chang) my drink is the wine of wisdom's nectar Mil. — Here the process of brewing may be mentioned. When the boiled barley (Ld. *sbo-bód* (sbo bod), Ts. *ṭab* (thab)) has grown cold, some *pabs* (phabs) (q.v.) is added, after which it is left standing for two or three days, until fermentation commences, when it is called glum (glum). Having sufficiently fermented, some water is poured to it, and the beer is considered to be ready for use. If proper care is taken (and the people of Ü and Ladak generally do so), the pale beer, thus obtained, is not amiss, and sparkles a good deal, but not being hopped it does not keep long. The people of Lahoul are accustomed to press out the glum (glum) with their hands, instead of filtering it, and mismanage the business also in other respects, so that their čań (chang) is a gray muddy liquor, that has hardly any resemblance to beer. The residue of malt, called sbáń-ma (sbang ma), may be mixed with water or milk, pressed through a strainer, and used instead of barm in baking bread, cakes etc. Comp. čań-kań (chang khang) beer-house, pot-house, tavern. — čań-ṭań (chang ṭang) drinking-cup or bowl Sch., Wts. — čań-čem-čań (chang chem can) an intoxicated person. — čań-čem-sa (chang chem sa) Lex. prob. = čań-sa (chang sa). — čań-tuń-mkan (chang thung mkhan) a beer-drinker; *čań-tuń-kan (chang thung khan) máń-po (mang po) dzom* ('dzom) a great beer-drinking bout takes place W. — *čań-dad-čań* (chang dad can) a drunkard, tippler W. — čań-tsúgs (chang tshugs) = čań-čan (chang can) Sch. — čań-tsoń-gi (chang tshong gi) kyim (khyim) beer-house Dzl. — čań-sa (chang sa) 1. beer-house 2. beer-carousal, čań-sa (chang sa) čén-po (chen po) byéd-pa (byed pa) to give or arrange a great beer-drinking bout Mil.

1. also čad-dón (chad don), čad-mdó (chad mdo), W. *čád-ka* (chad ka), promise, engagement, agreement ka-čád (kha chad) oral, verbal engagement, lag-čád (lag chad) pledge of faith by hand; čad-dón (chad don) byéd-pa (byed pa), *čád-ka (chad ka) čò-če (co ce), zúm-če* (zum ce) W., to give a promise, make a contract; gtóń-(bai) (gtong bai) čad(-don) (chad don) byéd-pa (byed pa) to agree about giving; čad-dón (chad don) ltar (ltar) byéd-pa (byed pa) to keep, fulfil a promise; čad-rdó (chad rdo) 1. the stone which is broken in the ceremony of rdo (rdo) gcóg-pa (gcog pa) q.v. 2. monument, memorial of a covenant. — 2. in compounds also for čád-pa (chad pa) punishment, lus-čád (lus chad) corporeal punishment.
